title: Space Tech Briefing — May 24, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space explorers! And welcome to your daily dose of cosmic news, live from the Space Technology Briefing Room! It's Saturday, May 24, 2026, and we're kicking off the weekend with some fascinating updates from the final frontier and beyond.

First up, let's recap a major recent development that kept us all on the edge of our seats. Just yesterday, May 23rd, we saw SpaceX conduct the 12th test flight of its Starship launcher. This time, it was the highly anticipated V3 version, featuring the latest evolution of the Raptor engine and significant upgrades across numerous subsystems. The launch, from a new pad at Starbase, aimed high. The primary goal for the SuperHeavy Booster was to execute a successful liftoff, ascent, stage separation, a return-phase ignition, and a splashdown in the Gulf of Mexico. This test specifically bypassed the tricky return-to-launch-site recovery by the launch tower's arms. While truly groundbreaking, early reports indicate that this first flight of Starship V3 didn't quite achieve all its objectives. The teams are undoubtedly analyzing the data, and we'll bring you more as soon as we have it!

And speaking of groundbreaking, let's shift gears to an inspiring educational initiative. On May 22nd, a massive scientific endeavor kicked off right here on Earth! We're talking about the ChlorISS experiment, a fantastic project that saw 260,000 students from 4,500 classes dive into the world of plant biology. Co-led by astronaut Sophie Adenot, this experiment is designed to help students discover firsthand how gravity and light influence plant growth. It's a brilliant way to engage the next generation of scientists and engineers with real-world space research, right from their classrooms!

That's all for your morning briefing today, folks! Thank you for joining us. Keep looking up, keep exploring, and have a fantastic weekend. We'll be back on Monday with more exciting news from the universe. Stay tuned!
